nativeRange.habitat: Echinodorus 'Rose' is a hybrid cultivar with no wild population of its own. The parent genus Echinodorus is native to wetlands of the tropical Americas, including marshes, riverbanks, and seasonally flooded lowlands.

decorPreferences: moderate to bright lighting for best coloration

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo of Rose Sword (Echinodorus sp. Rose) from Modern Aquarium's listing. Shows the characteristic lance-shaped leaves with pink-rose new growth. Retail product image; copyright holder is Modern Aquarium.

summary: The Echinodorus Rose (Echinodorus sp. 'Rose') is a hybrid sword plant prized for new growth that emerges in shades of pink to rose before maturing to bright or dark green. It is generally considered easy to keep and well suited to medium or large planted aquariums.
careNotes: The Echinodorus Rose is a HYBRID cultivar of unknown specific parentage, sold in the aquarium trade under the names Echinodorus sp. 'Rose' and 'Rose Sword', with no recognised wild population of its own. Its parent genus, Echinodorus (family Alismataceae), is native to wetlands of the tropical Americas. Adults reach roughly 25–50 cm (10–20 in); under strong light and a nutrient-rich substrate the plant grows into a large background specimen, while lower light tends to keep it more compact. Brighter light also enhances the signature pink and rose coloration of new growth and produces a tighter, upright habit, whereas low light is tolerated but generally results in paler leaves and a looser form. This is a HEAVY ROOT FEEDER: plant in a deep, nutrient-rich substrate or aquarium soil, supplement with root tabs placed near the root zone (not directly under the crown), and keep the crown — the central growing point — just above the substrate surface at all times. A comfortable temperature range is 20–28 °C, consistent with most Echinodorus cultivars. Position it as a background plant in medium to large tanks, or as a midground specimen in extra-tall aquariums where its height suits that role. Newly purchased plants may shed their emersed (above-water) leaves once submerged before producing the longer, lance-shaped underwater form — this transition is normal and not a sign of poor health. Avoid sudden, drastic swings in water chemistry, particularly a rapid shift from soft and acidic to hard and alkaline conditions, as this tends to increase leaf melt.
breedingNotes: The Echinodorus Rose propagates vegetatively by sending out runners that develop daughter plants at their tips. Once each plantlet has formed a small rosette and put out visible roots, it can be separated from the runner and replanted elsewhere in the aquarium. Mature specimens may also produce adventitious plantlets — small offshoots that develop directly on long, emergent flower stalks — and can be divided at the crown if the plant has grown large enough to have multiple growing points. As a hybrid cultivar, seed propagation is not a practical option; runner propagation is the standard method and is generally straightforward, though runners may need periodic trimming if spread needs to be managed.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium — Rose Sword (Echinodorus sp. Rose)
primary.notes: Manifest-specified primary source. Confirmed temperature 68–82 °F (20–28 °C), moderate light (brighter light enhances coloration and tighter growth), CO2 not required, medium growth rate, background placement, runner propagation, heavy root feeder needing root tabs (not directly on crown), iron/micronutrient supplementation for deeper colour, easy care level. Common name 'Rose Sword'.

additional.name: Buce Plant — Echinodorus Rose
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirmed family Alismataceae, origin 'Tropical Western Hemisphere', size 10–20 in (25–50 cm), pH 5.0–5.8 (Buce's preferred display range), moderate lighting (higher light enhances coloration), CO2 optional, medium growth rate, mid- or background placement, runner propagation, heavy root feeder requiring nutrient-rich substrate / aquarium soil, easy care level. Notes new foliage emerges pink/bronze and matures to bright or dark green.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Echinodorus
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy (family Alismataceae, order Alismatales) and confirmation that Echinodorus species/hybrids are American wetland marsh/bog plants that grow submersed, and that named forms in the aquarium trade frequently arise from artificial hybridisation — supporting treatment of 'Rose' as a trade cultivar without a single wild origin.
